1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.
159

1	# Prover — Prover Stage
2	
3	You fill `sorry` placeholders with complete proofs in your assigned `.lean` file.
4	
5	## Workflow
6	
7	1. Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
8	2. Read `task_pending.md` for prior attempts, dead ends, relevant lemmas on your file.
9	3. Check the `.lean` file for `/- USER: ... -/` comments (file-specific user hints).
10	4. **Read the relevant blueprint chapter before writing Lean code.** The chapter holds the mathematical proof sketch you must align with. When stuck, re-reading the blueprint is often the fastest path forward.
11	5. Replace `sorry` with Lean proofs. Push as far as possible.
12	6. **Always save partial progress in the code.** If you can't fully close a sorry, leave your best attempt — commented-out steps, helper lemmas, partial `by` blocks with `sorry` at the stuck point. The file must still compile (use scoped `sorry` if needed), but your work must be visible for the next agent to continue from. NEVER revert to a bare `sorry` — that wastes the work.
13	7. Write your results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
14	
15	**Write permissions**: only your assigned `.lean` file(s) and `task_results/<your_file>.md`. Do NOT edit `PROGRESS.md`, `task_pending.md`, `task_done.md`, blueprint chapters, or other agents' files.
16	
17	## Know your target in the dependency graph (leandag)
18	
19	The blueprint dependency graph is queryable **read-only** — `archon` is on PATH, `--json` is parseable (the banner goes to stderr). Use it to ground your work instead of guessing:
20	
21	- `archon dag-query node --node <label> --json` — your target's blueprint entry, its `\lean{}` pin, and status. **If the `\lean{}` pin is a `…TODO.…` placeholder, the Lean declaration does not exist yet** — you must *create* it (the typed `sorry`, or the proof if your mode builds it), not "fill" a name that isn't in the environment.
22	- `archon dag-query ancestors --node <label> --json` — your target's full dependency closure. Build on deps that are actually done (`\leanok`); a dependency still marked ∞ / `sorry` is **not** a sound foundation — cite it only if it genuinely typechecks today.
23	- `archon dag-query gaps --json` — the ∞ holes (statements with no informal proof). If your target's cone hits one, the blueprint sketch is incomplete: flag it in your task_result rather than inventing a proof to paper over it.
24	- `archon dag-query unmatched --json` — Lean decls with no blueprint entry. After you add helpers, your new names surface here until they're blueprinted — that is the debt you must report (see Logging → "Needs blueprint entry").
25	
26	The label↔Lean mapping is the `\lean{}` annotation in the blueprint block; `leandag stats` / `leandag focus` also give a project-wide view. You never *write* the graph — that's the dag/plan/review agents' job — but reading it keeps you from proving against a hollow or unfinished foundation.
27	
28	## Protected declarations
29	
30	`archon-protected.yaml` lists declarations with **frozen signatures**. You may fill their proof bodies but must not rename, re-type, reorder arguments, or weaken hypotheses. Only the mathematician edits protected signatures.
31	
32	## Talking to the user (TO_USER.md)
33	
34	`.archon/TO_USER.md` is a *persistent* shared notice board surfaced to the user as a banner. You may add a bullet **only** for something the user genuinely must act on that you hit while proving — e.g. a missing credential/dependency a sorry is gated on, or a `/- USER: -/` question you cannot resolve. Discipline:
35	
36	- **Concise + relevant**: keep the whole file ≤ 2–3 short bullets; before adding, read it and delete any bullet no longer true. Never a question queue — the loop never waits.
37	- **Concurrency**: provers run in parallel (one per file). To avoid clobbering a sibling lane's bullet, append your single bullet prefixed with your file (`- [Foo/Bar.lean] …`) rather than rewriting the whole file, and only when it's truly user-actionable. Routine "couldn't close this sorry" notes go in `task_results/<your_file>.md`, NOT here.
38	
39	## Avoid early termination
40	
41	- Don't abandon a proof prematurely. Many complex proofs run to thousands of lines.
42	- Difficulty is NOT a valid reason to leave a `sorry`.
43	- Don't delegate to "the next iteration" or "another prover" if more effort could close it.
44	- Only modify the proof for your assigned task — leave unrelated proofs untouched.
45	- **Decompose**: break the proof into smaller sub-problems (following the blueprint's lemma structure when available: L1, L2, L3, …) and solve each individually until the entire goal closes.
46	
47	## Task completion criteria
48	
49	Your task is complete ONLY when ALL of:
50	
51	1. Every `sorry` in scope is replaced with a complete proof.
52	2. Zero axioms introduced.
53	3. The file compiles cleanly.
54	
55	## Never weaken the type to dodge the proof
56	
57	When the substantive type is unattainable this iter, you have one honest move: leave `sorry` with the **intended type signature** so the type itself encodes the claim. You must NOT substitute a tautologically-true type to trivialize the body. Three patterns are banned:
58	
59	- **Reflexive-iso placeholder.** Replacing a non-trivial `X ≅ Y` (or `Nonempty (X ≅ Y)`) target with `Nonempty (X ≅ X) := ⟨Iso.refl _⟩`. The type now asserts nothing; the docstring's "iter-N placeholder" disclosure does not undo the structural lie, and the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference will resolve to this hollow declaration.
60	- **`Classical.choice` body around an explicit witness.** Writing `Classical.choice (α := X) ⟨witness⟩` to dissolve `Type` vs `Prop` friction — the witness is unreachable through any unfold, so the def is indistinguishable from `Classical.choice ⟨any-other-X⟩` downstream. Either use `Classical.choose` on the existential chain (preserves the spec), or `Nonempty.some`-with-witness; or ship a typed `sorry`.
61	- **Empty-content `proof_wanted`.** `proof_wanted` discards the declaration post-elaboration — the named decl never enters the environment, so the blueprint's `\lean{...}` cross-reference breaks. Use `theorem name : T := by sorry` with the intended signature instead.
62	
63	**Litmus test**: if you `unfold` your declaration, does it expose the named substantive content (Kähler differential module, the explicit iso, …) or does it stop at `Classical.choice` / `Iso.refl _` / nothing? If the latter, the body is structurally vacuous — ship the typed `sorry` instead.
64	
65	## When infrastructure is missing
66	
67	Do NOT report "Mathlib lacks X" and stop. Before giving up:
68	
69	1. **Use the informal agent** (`.claude/tools/archon-informal-agent.py`): "Prove [goal] without using [missing infrastructure], only Mathlib." Even an imperfect sketch is valuable.
70	2. **Try the alternative** — formalize whatever the informal agent suggests.
71	3. **If you still can't**: write the alternative sketch to `informal/<theorem_name>.md` and record in your task result what you tried, why it failed, AND the alternative route you found. "I couldn't prove X, but here's approach Y that might work because Z" is far more useful than "infrastructure missing".
72	
73	When stuck more generally: break into smaller subgoals, search Mathlib more thoroughly, prove missing helpers yourself, try alternative strategies, re-read the blueprint, use Web Search for published proofs.
74	
75	**Impossibility vs difficulty**: technical difficulty → keep trying. Mathematical impossibility → immediately backtrack and document why.
76	
77	## Proof style
78	
79	- **Never modify working proofs** — if a declaration has no `sorry` and compiles, do not touch its body.
80	- Keep edits minimal; don't delete comments or change labels; don't add unrelated declarations.
81	- Helper lemmas you introduced may be modified if they turn out wrong.
82	- Add a concise comment above each helper lemma so reuse is easy.
83	- **List every new declaration you introduce under a `## Needs blueprint entry` heading in your task_result** (name, file, and the facts its proof relies on) — see Logging below. The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: the review agent and planner consume this list (and `archon dag-query unmatched`) to give each new declaration a blueprint block. An unreported helper is an invisible, isolated dependency that silently corrupts the frontier — flagging it is mandatory, not optional. (You never write the blueprint yourself; you only name what needs one.)
84	- **`change` vs `show`** — `change` reshapes the goal up to defeq; `show` is purely display-level annotation. Using `show` where `change` is needed produces a linter warning. Default to `change` when in doubt.

6	import Mathlib

8	/-!
9	# Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf (`i = 0`)
10	
11	This file establishes the `i = 0` (direct-image) case of flat base change: the
12	formation of the pushforward `f_* F` of a quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat
13	base change on the target.
14	
15	Throughout we work with a (commutative, eventually cartesian) square of schemes
16	```
17	  X' --g'--> X
18	  |f'        |f
19	  v          v
20	  S' --g---> S
21	```
22	recorded by morphisms `f : X ⟶ S`, `g : S' ⟶ S`, `f' : X' ⟶ S'`, `g' : X' ⟶ X`
23	with `g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`, and `F : X.Modules` a sheaf of modules on `X`.
24	
25	The three main declarations are:
26	
27	* `AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap` — the canonical base-change map
28	  `g^*(f_* F) ⟶ f'_*((g')^* F)`, built as the adjoint mate of the unit of the
29	  `((g')^*, (g')_*)`-adjunction.
30	* `AlgebraicGeometry.affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` — for `f` affine and the
31	  square cartesian, the base-change map is an isomorphism (affine case: tensor
32	  associativity).
33	* `AlgebraicGeometry.flatBaseChange_pushforward_isIso` — for `g` flat and `f`
34	  quasi-compact quasi-separated, the base-change map is an isomorphism.
35	
36	See `blueprint/src/chapters/Cohomology_FlatBaseChange.tex`.
37	
38	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §"Cohomology and base change, I",
39	Tag 02KH.
40	-/
41	
42	universe u
43	
44	open CategoryTheory Limits
45	
46	namespace AlgebraicGeometry
47	
48	open Scheme.Modules
49	
50	variable {S S' X X' : Scheme.{u}}
51	  (f : X ⟶ S) (g : S' ⟶ S) (f' : X' ⟶ S') (g' : X' ⟶ X)
52	
53	/-- The canonical base-change map for the pushforward of a sheaf of modules.
54	
55	Given a commutative square
56	```
57	  X' --g'--> X
58	  |f'        |f
59	  v          v
60	  S' --g---> S
61	```
62	(with `comm : g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g`) and a sheaf of modules `F` on `X`, this is the
63	canonical morphism `g^*(f_* F) ⟶ f'_*((g')^* F)` of sheaves of modules on `S'`.
64	
65	It is the image, under the `(g^*, g_*)`-adjunction transpose, of the composite
66	```
67	  f_* F --f_*(unit)--> f_* (g')_* (g')^* F
68	        = (g' ≫ f)_* (g')^* F = (f' ≫ g)_* (g')^* F = g_* f'_* (g')^* F,
69	```
70	where `unit` is the unit of the `((g')^*, (g')_*)`-adjunction and the middle
71	equalities are the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward together with the
72	commutativity of the square.
73	
74	Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, §"Cohomology and base change, I",
75	base-change diagram. -/
76	noncomputable def pushforwardBaseChangeMap (comm : g' ≫ f = f' ≫ g) (F : X.Modules) :
77	    (Scheme.Modules.pullback g).obj ((pushforward f).obj F) ⟶
78	      (pushforward f').obj ((Scheme.Modules.pullback g').obj F) :=
79	  ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g).homEquiv _ _).symm
80	    ((pushforward f).map ((pullbackPushforwardAdjunction g').unit.app F) ≫
81	      (pushforwardComp g' f).hom.app _ ≫
82	      (pushforwardCongr comm).hom.app _ ≫
83	      (pushforwardComp f' g).inv.app _)
84	
85	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — locality of isomorphisms for `Scheme.Modules`
86	
87	Mathlib provides the per-open criterion `Scheme.Modules.Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app`
88	(`IsIso φ ↔ ∀ U, IsIso (φ.app U)`) and the stalkwise criterion
89	`TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso` for `TopCat.Sheaf`-valued morphisms,
90	but it does not package the stalk-local criterion at the level of `Scheme.Modules`
91	morphisms. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they are the locality tools needed for
92	the affine reduction of `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso` (where one checks the
93	base-change map after restricting to / taking stalks over affine opens). -/
94	
95	/-- **Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of `𝒪_X`-modules.** A morphism `φ : M ⟶ N`
96	of sheaves of modules over a scheme `X` is an isomorphism iff its underlying morphism of
97	abelian presheaves is a stalkwise isomorphism. Project-local: Mathlib only ships the
98	per-open criterion and the `TopCat.Sheaf`-level stalk criterion separately. -/
99	theorem Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
100	    (φ : M ⟶ N) :
101	    IsIso φ ↔ ∀ x : X, IsIso ((TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor Ab.{u} x).map
102	      ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ)) := by
103	  constructor
104	  · intro h x
105	    haveI : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ) := Functor.map_isIso _ _
106	    exact Functor.map_isIso _ _
107	  · intro h
108	    -- Package the underlying abelian presheaves as `TopCat.Sheaf`es.
109	    let MS : TopCat.Sheaf Ab.{u} X := ⟨M.presheaf, M.isSheaf⟩
110	    let NS : TopCat.Sheaf Ab.{u} X := ⟨N.presheaf, N.isSheaf⟩
111	    let fS : MS ⟶ NS := ⟨(Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ⟩
112	    haveI : ∀ x : X, IsIso ((TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor Ab.{u} x).map fS.hom) := h
113	    haveI hSiso : IsIso fS := TopCat.Presheaf.isIso_of_stalkFunctor_map_iso fS
114	    have h1 : IsIso ((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ) :=
115	      (TopCat.Sheaf.forget Ab.{u} X).map_isIso fS
116	    exact (CategoryTheory.isIso_iff_of_reflects_iso φ (Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X)).mp h1
117	
118	/-- **Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of `𝒪_X`-modules.** If `B` is a basis of
119	opens of `X` and `φ : M ⟶ N` restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every basic
120	open `B i`, then `φ` is an isomorphism. This reduces iso-checking from *all* opens (the
121	content of `Scheme.Modules.Hom.isIso_iff_isIso_app`) to a chosen basis. Project-local:
122	Mathlib provides the stalkwise pieces (`germ_exist_of_isBasis`,
123	`stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis`) but not the packaged criterion at the
124	`Scheme.Modules` level. -/
125	theorem Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
126	    {ι : Type*} {B : ι → X.Opens} (hB : TopologicalSpace.Opens.IsBasis (Set.range B))
127	    (φ : M ⟶ N) (h : ∀ i, IsIso (φ.app (B i))) : IsIso φ := by
128	  -- Reduce to a stalkwise isomorphism of the underlying `Ab`-presheaf morphism `α`.
129	  rw [Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map]
130	  intro x
131	  -- `α.app (op (B i))` is definitionally `φ.app (B i)`, hence an isomorphism on each basic open.
132	  have happ : ∀ U ∈ Set.range B,
133	      IsIso (((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ).app (Opposite.op U)) := by
134	    rintro U ⟨i, rfl⟩; exact h i
135	  rw [CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.isIso_iff_bijective]
136	  refine ⟨?_, ?_⟩
137	  · -- Injectivity of the stalk map from injectivity on a basis.
138	    refine TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor_map_injective_of_isBasis hB ?_ x
139	    intro U hU
140	    haveI := happ U hU
141	    exact (CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso
142	      (((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ).app (Opposite.op U))).injective
143	  · -- Surjectivity: a germ at `x` comes from a section over a basic open, where `α` is onto.
144	    intro t
145	    obtain ⟨U, hxU, hU, s, rfl⟩ :=
146	      TopCat.Presheaf.germ_exist_of_isBasis hB N.presheaf x t
147	    haveI := happ U hU
148	    obtain ⟨s', hs'⟩ := (CategoryTheory.ConcreteCategory.bijective_of_isIso
149	      (((Scheme.Modules.toPresheaf X).map φ).app (Opposite.op U))).surjective s
150	    refine ⟨M.presheaf.germ U x hxU s', ?_⟩
151	    erw [TopCat.Presheaf.stalkFunctor_map_germ_apply]
152	    rw [hs']
153	    rfl
154	
155	/-- **Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of `𝒪_X`-modules.** A morphism
156	`φ : M ⟶ N` of sheaves of modules is an isomorphism iff it restricts to an isomorphism on
157	the sections over every affine open of `X`. This is the precise reduction used in the
158	affine proof of `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso`: the affine opens of `X` form a basis,
159	and over an affine open the base-change map becomes the pure ring-theoretic
160	`cancelBaseChange` isomorphism. -/
161	theorem Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens {X : Scheme.{u}} {M N : X.Modules}
162	    (φ : M ⟶ N) : IsIso φ ↔ ∀ U : X.affineOpens, IsIso (φ.app U) := by
163	  refine ⟨fun _ U => inferInstance, fun h => ?_⟩
164	  exact Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis
165	    (B := (Subtype.val : X.affineOpens → X.Opens))
166	    (by simpa [Subtype.range_val] using X.isBasis_affineOpens) φ h

168	/-! ## Project-local Mathlib supplement — affine tilde dictionary (global sections)

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
4	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
5	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
6	
7	\section{Setup and motivation}
8	
9	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
10	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
11	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
12	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
13	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
14	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
15	out of scope here.
16	
17	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
18	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
19	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
20	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
21	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
22	\[
23	  \begin{array}{ccc}
24	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
25	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
26	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
27	  \end{array}
28	\]
29	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
30	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
31	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
32	
33	\section{The canonical base-change map}
34	
35	\begin{definition}\leanok
36	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
37	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
38	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
39	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
40	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
41	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
42	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
43	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
44	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
45	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
46	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
47	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
48	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
49	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
50	  change, I.}
51	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
52	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
53	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
54	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
55	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
56	  \[
57	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
58	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
59	  \]
60	  It is adjoint to the morphism
61	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
62	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
63	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
64	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
65	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
66	\end{definition}

68	\section{The affine case}
69	
70	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for \texttt{Scheme.Modules} morphisms}
71	
72	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
73	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
74	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
75	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
76	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
77	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
78	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
79	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
80	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
81	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
82	
83	\begin{lemma}\leanok
84	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
85	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
86	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
87	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
88	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
89	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
90	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
91	\end{lemma}
92	
93	\begin{proof}
94	
95	
96	\leanok
97	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
98	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
99	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
100	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
101	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
102	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
103	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
104	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
105	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
106	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
107	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
108	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
109	\end{proof}
110	
111	\begin{lemma}\leanok
112	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
113	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
114	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
115	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
116	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
117	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
118	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
119	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
120	  isomorphism.
121	\end{lemma}
122	
123	\begin{proof}
124	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
125	  \leanok
126	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
127	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
128	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
129	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
130	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
131	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
132	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
133	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
134	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
135	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
136	\end{proof}
137	
138	\begin{lemma}\leanok
139	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
140	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
142	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
143	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
144	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
145	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
146	\end{lemma}
147	
148	\begin{proof}
149	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
150	  \leanok
151	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
152	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
153	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
154	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
155	  affine opens.
156	\end{proof}
157	
158	\subsection{The affine computation}
159	
160	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
161	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
162	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
163	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
164	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
165	
166	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
167	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
168	their proof sketches.
169	
170	\begin{lemma}
171	
172	\leanok
173	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
174	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
175	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
176	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
177	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
178	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
179	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
180	  \[
181	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
182	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
183	  \]
184	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
185	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
186	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
187	\end{lemma}
188	
189	\begin{proof}
190	
191	
192	\leanok
193	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
194	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
195	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
196	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
197	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
198	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
199	\end{proof}
200	
201	\begin{lemma}
202	
203	\leanok
204	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
205	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
206	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
207	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
208	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
209	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
210	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
211	  \[
212	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
213	    \;\cong\;
214	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
215	  \]
216	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
217	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
218	  of \(N\).
219	\end{lemma}
220	
221	\begin{proof}
222	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
223	  \leanok
224	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
225	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
226	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
227	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
228	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
229	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
230	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
231	  equation of
232	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
233	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
234	\end{proof}
235	
236	\begin{lemma}
237	
238	\leanok
239	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
240	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
241	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
242	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
243	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
244	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
245	  \[
246	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
247	    \;\cong\;
248	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
249	  \]
250	\end{lemma}
251	
252	\begin{proof}
253	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
254	  \leanok
255	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
256	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
257	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
258	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
259	\end{proof}
260	
261	\begin{lemma}
262	
263	\leanok
264	[Sections of an affine pushforward over an arbitrary open]
265	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
266	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
267	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
268	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, let \(N\) be
269	  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module, and let \(U\) be an open of
270	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), with preimage
271	  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(U)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\). Then
272	  there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
273	  \[
274	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
275	    \;\cong\;
276	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) .
277	  \]
278	  This is the open-indexed generalization of
279	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, which is the case \(U = \top\) (so that
280	  \(V = \top\) as well). The family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of these isomorphisms is moreover
281	  natural in the open argument: the structure-sheaf restriction maps on the two
282	  sides commute with the isomorphisms, so the family is a natural isomorphism of the
283	  two presheaves of \(R\)-modules in the open variable (this naturality is
284	  established in the proof below). For \(U = D(a)\) it is exactly the linear
285	  equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement~(1) in the proof of
286	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
287	\end{lemma}
288	
289	\begin{proof}
290	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
291	  \leanok
292	  The construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim,
293	  with the top open \(\top\) replaced throughout by the arbitrary open \(U\) (and
294	  its preimage \(V\)). The point that makes this replacement free is that the
295	  \(R\)-module structure on the sections is supplied \emph{uniformly} by
296	  restriction of scalars along the global-sections ring map at the top open,
297	  independently of the evaluation open; consequently both sides unwind to the same
298	  nested restriction-of-scalars towers, reconciled by applying the
299	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the
300	  \(\top\)-level ring equation of
301	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. Naturality in the open
302	  follows because every constituent of the construction is either a structure-sheaf
303	  restriction map or conjugation by a fixed ring map, and all of these commute with
304	  further restriction along an inclusion of opens.
305	
306	  \medskip
307	  \emph{Naturality of the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) in the open (remark).} The family is
308	  in fact a natural isomorphism between two presheaves of \(R\)-modules on
309	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\): the source presheaf
310	  \(U \mapsto \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)\), with the
311	  structure-sheaf restriction maps of the pushforward, and the target presheaf
312	  \(U \mapsto
313	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U)\bigr)\),
314	  with the structure-sheaf restriction maps of \(N\) transported through the preimage
315	  operation \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) and read as \(R\)-linear maps by
316	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). Concretely, for any inclusion of opens
317	  \(U' \subseteq U\), with \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U\) and
318	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U'\), the square with horizontal maps
319	  \(e_U, e_{U'}\) and vertical maps the respective structure-sheaf restrictions
320	  commutes. The reason is structural: each \(e_U\) is a composite whose constituents
321	  are of exactly two kinds — structure-sheaf restriction maps (of \(N\),
322	  respectively of \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\)) and identity-on-carrier
323	  \(\operatorname{restrictScalars}\) repackagings (conjugation by the \emph{fixed},
324	  open-independent ring map supplied by the restriction-of-scalars reconciliation and
325	  by the \(\top\)-level ring equation of
326	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}). Restriction maps commute
327	  with further restriction by the presheaf functoriality axiom (using
328	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U' \subseteq
329	  (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U = V\), as \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) is
330	  monotone), and conjugation by a fixed ring map commutes with restriction because the
331	  ring map does not depend on \(U\); the naturality square is the paste of these
332	  component squares, and each \(e_U\) being an isomorphism makes the family a natural
333	  isomorphism. This open-naturality is exactly the compatibility invoked, at the
334	  inclusion \(D(a) \subseteq \top\), in the proof of
335	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.
336	\end{proof}

338	\begin{lemma}
339	
340	\leanok
341	[The tilde restriction from the top open to a basic open is a localization]
342	  \label{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
343	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}

345	  restriction map
346	  \[
347	    \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top) \longrightarrow \Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(b)),
348	  \]

354

356	
357	\leanok
358	  By the defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), the tilde-localization map
359	  \(M \to \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(b))\) exhibits its target as the localization of
360	  \(M\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(b)\). The global-sections map

370	We record three further bespoke supports that drive the basic-open verification of
371	the affine-pushforward isomorphism below. They carry no external citation and stand
372	on their proof sketches.
373	
374	\begin{lemma}
375	
376	\leanok
377	[Restriction of scalars of a localized module]
378	  \label{lem:powers_restrictScalars}
379	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.IsLocalizedModule.powers_restrictScalars}
380	  Let \(A\) be a commutative \(R\)-algebra, \(S \subseteq R\) a submonoid, and
381	  \(f : M \to N\) an \(A\)-linear map that exhibits \(N\) as the localization of
382	  \(M\) at the image submonoid
383	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S) \subseteq A\). Then the underlying \(R\)-linear map
384	  \(\operatorname{restr}_R f\) exhibits \(N\) as the localization of \(M\) at \(S\)
385	  itself.

387	
388	\begin{proof}
389	
390	
391	\leanok
392	  This is the exact converse of the standard fact that a localization of \(M\) at a
393	  submonoid \(S\) of \(R\) remains a localization after enlarging the base to an
394	  \(R\)-algebra \(A\) along the image submonoid \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\). One
395	  checks the three defining conditions of a localized module directly. Each
396	  \(s \in S\) acts invertibly on \(N\) because its image
397	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) does and the two actions coincide by the scalar
398	  tower \(R \to A \to N\). Surjectivity of \(f\) up to denominators and the
399	  equality-witness condition transport along the map
400	  \(s \mapsto \operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\) from \(S\) onto
401	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(S)\), again using
402	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_A(s)\cdot{} = s\cdot{}\). In the application
403	  \(A = R'\), \(S = \mathrm{powers}(a)\), one has
404	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\), so
405	  this identifies the localization of \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at \(a\)
406	  with the localization of \(M\) at \(\varphi a\); it is the ring-change core of the

408	\end{proof}

410	\begin{lemma}
411	
412	\leanok
413	[Section-level counit isomorphism from a localization hypothesis]
414	  \label{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
415	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.fromTildeΓ_app_isIso_of_isLocalizedModule}
416	  Let \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-module and \(a \in R\). Suppose the
417	  structure-sheaf restriction map \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\), read as
418	  an \(R\)-linear map of the global-section modules, exhibits \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as
419	  the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then the
420	  tilde--\(\Gamma\) counit

422	  isomorphism on the sections over the basic open \(D(a)\).
423	\end{lemma}
424	
425	\begin{proof}
426	
427	
428	\leanok
429	  Over \(D(a)\) the section map \(L\) of the counit sits in the triangle identity
430	  \(L \circ j = \rho\), where
431	  \(j : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma\bigl(\widetilde{\Gamma(N,\top)}, D(a)\bigr)\) is
432	  the tilde-localization map and \(\rho : \Gamma(N,\top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) is

435	  defining property of \(\widetilde{(-)}\), and \(\rho\) by hypothesis. By the
436	  uniqueness of localized modules there is a unique \(R\)-linear isomorphism \(e\)

439	  criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}.)
440	\end{proof}
441	
442	\begin{lemma}
443	
444	\leanok
445	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module, conditional form]
446	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional}
447	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_of_isLocalizedModule}
448	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
449	  lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
450	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and \(M\) an
451	  \(R'\)-module. Suppose that for every \(a \in R\) the structure-sheaf restriction
452	  of the pushforward \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) exhibits its
453	  sections over \(D(a)\) as the localization of its global sections at
454	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
455	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
456	  \[
457	    (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M \;\cong\;
458	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)} .
459	  \]
460	\end{lemma}
461	
462	\begin{proof}
463	  \leanok
464	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized}
465	  Write \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\). Under the hypothesis,
466	  Lemma~\ref{lem:fromTildeGamma_app_isIso_of_localized} applies for each \(a \in R\),
467	  so the counit \(\mathrm{fromTilde}\Gamma\,N\) restricts to an isomorphism on the
468	  sections over every basic open \(D(a)\). Since the basic opens form a basis of
469	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), the basis-local criterion

517	\begin{proof}
518	  \leanok
519	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars, lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
520	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
521	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
522	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
523	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
524	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
525	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
526	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
527	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
528	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
529	
530	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
531	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
532	  \[
533	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
534	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
535	  \]
536	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
537	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
538	
539	  \medskip
540	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
541	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
542	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
543	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
544	
545	  \medskip
546	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
547	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
548	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
549	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
550	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
551	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
552	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
553	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
554	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
555	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
556	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
557	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
558	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
559	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
560	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
561	  an isomorphism.
562	
563	  \medskip
564	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
565	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
566	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
567	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
568	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
569	  \(R[1/a]\). The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
570	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
571	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
572	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
573	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
574	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). The \(R\)-action on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) is the honest
575	  restriction-of-scalars action carried by the scalar tower \(R \to R' \to {-}\)
576	  determined by \(\varphi\); against that structure the ring-change converse
577	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applicable, so the carrier of the
578	  \(R\)-module is exactly the one to which the localization claim refers, and no
579	  recursively-defined or ad-hoc section-level scalar action intervenes.
580	  We obtain \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the already-known \(R'\)-side
581	  localization (Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}), in
582	  element-free fashion, across the open-indexed section comparison of
583	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} \emph{using its naturality in the open}
584	  (the naturality remark in the proof of
585	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}). This proceeds in three
586	  movements.
587	
588	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} This is the component at
589	  \(U = D(a)\) of the open-indexed family of
590	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the \(R\)-linear isomorphism
591	  \[
592	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
593	      \;\cong\;
594	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) ,
595	  \]
596	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
597	  definitional. No fresh \(D(a)\)-level construction is needed: this is precisely the
598	  open-indexed isomorphism already supplied by
599	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} evaluated at the basic open \(D(a)\).
600	
601	  \emph{(2) The naturality square for \(D(a) \subseteq \top\).} The compatibility
602	  that relates \(e_{D(a)}\) to the \(\top\)-level isomorphism
603	  \(e_\top\) (the global-sections comparison of
604	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}) is precisely the open-naturality square of the
605	  family \(\{e_U\}_U\) (established in the proof of
606	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}), instantiated at the inclusion
607	  \(D(a) \subseteq \top\): the square
608	  \[
609	    \begin{array}{ccc}
610	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, \top\bigr)
611	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_\top\;}
612	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top)\bigr) \\[4pt]
613	      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}}\big\downarrow & &
614	        \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}} \\[4pt]
615	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
616	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{D(a)}\;}
617	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr)
618	    \end{array}
619	  \]
620	  commutes, using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
621	  definitional. Because the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) is a natural isomorphism, this single
622	  square is available uniformly in \(a\); there is no per-\(a\) section-level identity
623	  to re-prove by hand. It intertwines the source restriction
624	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) on the pushforward with the (restriction-of-scalars
625	  reading of the) target restriction \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}\) on
626	  \(\widetilde M\).
627	
628	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target restriction
629	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} : \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
630	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) =
631	  M[1/\varphi a]\) as the \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of
632	  \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) over \(R'\); this is exactly
633	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}. Apply the ring-change converse
634	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
635	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
636	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) —
637	  its \(\operatorname{Algebra} R\, R'\) and scalar-tower hypotheses being supplied by
638	  the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\) — to read this same map as
639	  the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\). The commuting naturality square
640	  of movement (2) intertwines this target restriction with the source restriction
641	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} : \Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) by way of the
642	  isomorphisms \(e_\top\) and \(e_{D(a)}\); transporting the localization property

644	  modules — yields that \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) is the
645	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\
646	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). No section-level computation specific to the individual \(a\)
647	  is performed: the only \(a\)-dependent input is the instantiation of the single
648	  natural-isomorphism square at \(D(a)\). Feeding the family
649	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
650	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
651	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\).
652	
653	  \medskip
654	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
655	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
656	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
657	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
658	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the naturality
659	  remark in the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the family
660	  \(\{e_U\}_U\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the
661	  structure-sheaf restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this
662	  open-naturality that drives the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the
663	  per-\(a\) localization fact \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level
664	  localization Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with that
665	  naturality square for the inclusion
666	  \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a section-level naturality square

668	  obligation of the construction; the carrier of the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
669	  sections is the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\), against which
670	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} applies directly.
671	
672	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
673	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest
674	  restriction-of-scalars structure: \(R\) acts through \(\varphi\), i.e.\ via the
675	  \(R\)-algebra structure on \(R'\) determined by \(\varphi\) and the associated
676	  scalar tower \(R \to R' \to M[1/\varphi a]\). It is \emph{not} an ad-hoc,
677	  recursively-defined scalar action. Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is
678	  stated against exactly this restriction-of-scalars / scalar-tower structure, and
679	  it is under that structure that localizing \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at
680	  \(a\) coincides with localizing \(M\) at \(\varphi a\).
681

683	  % a quasi-coherent sheaf along an affine morphism is quasi-coherent. In current

691	  exhibits \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M} \cong
692	  \widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) as the tilde of a module. Tilde
693	  modules are quasi-coherent and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
694	  is closed under isomorphism, so the pushforward
695	  \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent. This
696	  conclusion is stated \emph{after} the object isomorphism, of which it is a

701	\begin{remark}
702	  This single isomorphism discharges both inputs that the affine reduction below
703	  requires of the pushforward of a tilde-module.
704	  \begin{enumerate}
705	    \item \emph{The global-sections comparison.} Applying the global-sections
706	      (module-of-sections) functor to the isomorphism identifies the
707	      \(R\)-module of sections of
708	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) with
709	      \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\); this is exactly the \(\Gamma\)-fragment
710	      comparison used to recognise the section-level base-change map.
711	    \item \emph{Quasi-coherence of the pushforward.} The tilde of any module is
712	      quasi-coherent, and quasi-coherence of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules is
713	      closed under isomorphism; hence
714	      \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M}\) is quasi-coherent.
715	      In particular no separate ``pushforward preserves quasi-coherent'' theorem is
716	      needed for the affine lane.
717	  \end{enumerate}
718	  Because both inputs the affine reduction asks of the pushforward are corollaries
719	  of this single object isomorphism — the section-level module identification and
720	  the quasi-coherence of the pushforward — this lemma is the sole bespoke
721	  ingredient the affine lane requires.
722	\end{remark}

809	
810	The affine base-change lemma below proceeds \emph{directly on global sections}: it
811	reduces, via the locality criterion
812	Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} and the two affine dictionaries
813	Lemmas~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso} and~\ref{lem:pullback_spec_tilde_iso},
814	to two obligations that are isolated here as named blocks. Both are extracted from

815	the proof of Stacks ``Affine base change'', and both are absent from the pinned
816	Mathlib. The first is the \emph{locality reduction}: restricting to the affine opens
817	of \(S'\) reduces the isomorphism claim to the affine--affine model
818	\(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\), \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\),
819	\(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\), \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\). The second is the
820	\emph{section-level identification}: in that affine--affine model, the global-sections
821	incarnation \(\Gamma(\alpha)\) of the base-change map, transported through the two
822	proved tilde dictionaries, is the canonical cancellation isomorphism
823	\(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) — an isomorphism with no flatness hypothesis. This
824	is the honest cut of the affine close: locality on \(S'\), then a pure computation on
825	the module of global sections. No separate abstract adjoint-mate identification is
826	performed at the level of sheaves; the mate is unwound \emph{on sections}, where it
827	becomes \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\) directly.

850	  base-change map of the square obtained by restricting along \(U\). Consequently,
851	  by the affine-open locality criterion
852	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the assertion that the base-change
853	  map is an isomorphism over an arbitrary base-change square \((S, S', X, X')\)
854	  reduces to the affine--affine case \(S = \operatorname{Spec}(R)\),
855	  \(S' = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\), \(X = \operatorname{Spec}(A)\),
856	  \(\mathcal{F} = \widetilde{M}\) for an \(A\)-module \(M\).
857	
858	  \medskip
859	  \emph{Intended formalized signature.} With the ambient data
860	  \(f : X \to S\), \(g : S' \to S\), \(f' : X' \to S'\), \(g' : X' \to X\) and a
861	  proof \(h\) that the square is cartesian (\(h : \mathrm{IsPullback}\ g'\ f'\ f\ g\),
862	  whose commutativity \(h.w : g' \circ f = f' \circ g\) feeds the base-change map),
863	  under the hypotheses \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) and
864	  \([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}]\), the lemma is the locality reduction
865	  \[
866	    \bigl(\forall\, U : S'.\mathrm{affineOpens},\
867	      \mathrm{IsIso}\ \bigl((\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\ f\ g\ f'\ g'\ h.w\
868	        \mathcal{F}).\mathrm{app}\ U\bigr)\bigr)
869	    \;\Longrightarrow\;
870	    \mathrm{IsIso}\ \bigl(\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\ f\ g\ f'\ g'\ h.w\
871	      \mathcal{F}\bigr),
872	  \]
873	  i.e.\ it discharges the affine-open locality criterion
874	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} for the base-change map, with the
875	  per-affine-open hypothesis being exactly the affine--affine section assertion that
876	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange} supplies. The content
877	  beyond the bare locality criterion is the identification of the restriction of
878	  \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap} to an affine open \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R')
879	  \subseteq S'\) with the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out over a
880	  chosen affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing \(g(U)\), over which
881	  \([\mathrm{IsAffineHom}\ f]\) makes \(X\) restrict to \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\) and
882	  \([\mathcal{F}.\mathrm{IsQuasicoherent}]\) makes \(\mathcal{F}\) restrict to
883	  \(\widetilde{M}\).

886	\begin{proof}
887	  \uses{def:pushforward_base_change_map, lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
888	  By the affine-open locality criterion
889	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}, the morphism
890	  \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap} is an isomorphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
891	  as soon as its restriction to the sections over each affine open \(U \subseteq S'\)
892	  is an isomorphism. The only content beyond the bare criterion is to identify that
893	  restriction with the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out over
894	  \(U\). We obtain the identification by unfolding the definition of the map and
895	  pushing the restriction functor through each of its building blocks. The
896	  compatibility this needs is \emph{not} a new coherence theorem: it is the naturality
897	  of the adjunction transpose, together with the standard fact that pushforward
898	  commutes with restriction to an open of the base. We spell this out in three steps.
899	
900	  \medskip
901	  \emph{Step 1: unfold the map.} By
902	  Definition~\ref{def:pushforward_base_change_map}, \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
903	  is the image, under the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose — the natural bijection
904	  \(\operatorname{Hom}(g^*A, B) \cong \operatorname{Hom}(A, g_*B)\) — of the morphism
905	  \[
906	    f_*\mathcal{F} \xrightarrow{\,f_*(\eta_{\mathcal{F}})\,}
907	    f_*(g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F} = g_*\,f'_*(g')^*\mathcal{F},
908	  \]
909	  where \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}} : \mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) is the unit of
910	  the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction and the identification
911	  \(f_*(g')_* = g_*\,f'_*\) is the pseudofunctoriality of pushforward applied to the
912	  square's commutativity \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\). Thus the map is, by
913	  construction, the transpose of the \(f_*\)-image of a unit — a composite of
914	  adjunction data and pushforward functors, with no ingredient beyond these.
915	
916	  \medskip
917	  \emph{Step 2: restriction to \(U\) commutes with each building block.} Fix an affine
918	  open \(U \subseteq S'\) and write \(\rho_U\) for restriction to \(U\) —
919	  equivalently, the section functor \(\Gamma(U, -)\) on \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules,
920	  or pullback along the open immersion \(U \hookrightarrow S'\). Restriction along
921	  \(U\) is a functor, and every ingredient of Step 1 is a natural transformation or a
922	  natural bijection; so applying \(\rho_U\) to the transpose-of-a-unit of Step 1
923	  returns the transpose-of-the-unit built from the restricted data. Concretely:
924	  \begin{itemize}
925	    \item[(a)] the unit \(\eta_{\mathcal{F}}\) of the \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction
926	      restricts to the unit of the corresponding adjunction on the restricted square,
927	      because units are natural in their object and pulling the cartesian square back
928	      along \(U \hookrightarrow S'\) yields again a cartesian square (with total space
929	      the open \((f')^{-1}(U)\) of \(X'\));
930	    \item[(b)] \(f_*\) of a restricted morphism is the restriction of \(f_*\) of the
931	      morphism, because pushforward commutes with restriction to an open of the base:
932	      for an open \(U\) of the base one has
933	      \((f_*\mathcal{G})|_U = (f|_{f^{-1}U})_*\bigl(\mathcal{G}|_{f^{-1}U}\bigr)\),
934	      naturally in \(\mathcal{G}\) — and likewise for \((g')_*\) and \(f'_*\);
935	    \item[(c)] the \((g^*, g_*)\)-adjunction transpose is a bijection natural in both
936	      variables, so it commutes with restricting its source and target along \(U\).
937	  \end{itemize}
938	  Chaining (a), (b), (c) — i.e.\ transporting the transpose-of-unit composite of
939	  Step 1 through the restriction functor \(\rho_U\) and replacing each block by its
940	  restricted counterpart — shows that
941	  \((\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\dots).\mathrm{app}\,U\), the restriction of the
942	  base-change map to sections over \(U\), is itself \texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap},
943	  now formed from the cartesian square restricted over \(U\). Choosing an affine open
944	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R) \subseteq S\) containing the image \(g(U)\), affineness of
945	  \(f\) (local on the base and stable under base change) makes the total space
946	  restrict to an affine \(\operatorname{Spec}(A)\), and quasi-coherence of
947	  \(\mathcal{F}\) makes it restrict to a tilde-module \(\widetilde{M}\) there; so this
948	  restricted map is exactly the base-change map of the affine--affine square cut out
949	  over \(U = \operatorname{Spec}(R')\) and \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). The whole
950	  identification is thus definitional plus the naturalities (a)--(c); it introduces no
951	  obligation beyond them.
952	
953	  \medskip
954	  \emph{Step 3: conclude the reduction.} By Step 2 the per-affine-open hypothesis
955	  required by the locality criterion Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} —
956	  that \((\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\dots).\mathrm{app}\,U\) is an isomorphism
957	  for every affine open \(U \subseteq S'\) — is, under the identification of Step 2,
958	  precisely the affine--affine section assertion. That assertion is supplied, with
959	  \emph{no} flatness hypothesis, by the section-level computation
960	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange}, which identifies the
961	  affine--affine base-change map on global sections with the cancellation isomorphism
962	  \(\operatorname{cancelBaseChange}\). Applying the locality criterion
963	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens} to this family of per-open
964	  isomorphisms gives \(\mathrm{IsIso}(\texttt{pushforwardBaseChangeMap}\dots)\), which
965	  is the asserted locality reduction.
966	\end{proof}
